title: Computer Vision Deep Dive
description: Computer Vision from image classification to self-supervised learning — with FAANG interview depth.
details: CV techniques span image classification (ResNet, EfficientNet, ViT), object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN, DETR), image segmentation (U-Net, Mask R-CNN), face recognition (FaceNet, ArcFace), and self-supervised learning (SimCLR, MAE). This guide covers the architecture decisions, loss functions, and FAANG interview angles for each.

sections.title: Image Classification — ResNet, EfficientNet, ViT
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition: "ResNet's skip connection is the most important architectural innovation in CV — it let networks grow from 20 to 1000+ layers. Before ResNet, deeper always meant worse."

📊 Concrete: "ViT-L/16 (307M params) achieves 88.5% top-1 on ImageNet when pre-trained on JFT-300M. Without JFT pretraining, ResNet-152 (60M params) beats it. This tells you Transformers need more data."

Key Models:

  • AlexNet (Krizhevsky 2012): 5 conv + 3 FC, 60M params, ReLU, dropout, data augmentation. Won ImageNet by 10%+. First modern deep CNN.
  • VGG (Simonyan 2014): 16/19 layers, all 3×3 conv (stack of 2×3×3 replaces 5×5 → fewer params, more non-linearity). Simple but expensive (138M params, 19.6G FLOPs). Still used as a backbone for feature extraction.
  • ResNet (He 2015): skip connections (identity shortcut). x_{l+1} = x_l + F(x_l, W_l). Solves degradation problem (adding layers should not hurt training error). ResNet-152: 5.3% top-5 error, 11M params, 11.3G FLOPs. Won ImageNet 2015.
  • ResNeXt (Xie 2017): grouped convolutions (cardinality = 32 groups of 4 channels). More efficient than wider/deeper — increases capacity without FLOP explosion.
  • EfficientNet (Tan 2019): compound scaling — simultaneously scale depth, width, and resolution. Neural architecture search found the base. EfficientNet-B7: 84.3% top-1, 66M params, 37G FLOPs (VGG-level accuracy at ½ the params).
  • ViT (Dosovitskiy 2020): split image into 16×16 patches, treat as token sequence, apply Transformer. Needs massive data (JFT-300M) to outperform CNNs. Data-efficient ViT (DeiT) works on ImageNet-1K with distillation.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# ResNet-50 building block (Bottleneck): 1×1 → 3×3 → 1×1 conv with skip connection.
# The bottleneck reduces then expands channels for computational efficiency.

def bottleneck_block(x, filters, stride=1, downsample=False):
    """
    Args:
        x: input tensor (batch, H, W, C)
        filters: number of filters for the middle 3×3 conv
        stride: stride for the first 1×1 conv
        downsample: whether to project skip connection (dimension mismatch)
    """
    shortcut = x

    # 1×1 conv: reduce channels (bottleneck neck — entry)
    # e.g., 256 → 64 channels
    x = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(filters, (1, 1), strides=stride, padding='same')(x)
    x = tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()(x)
    x = tf.keras.layers.ReLU()(x)

    # 3×3 conv: spatial feature extraction at reduced dimension
    x = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(filters, (3, 3), strides=1, padding='same')(x)
    x = tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()(x)
    x = tf.keras.layers.ReLU()(x)

    # 1×1 conv: expand channels back (bottleneck neck — exit)
    # e.g., 64 → 256 channels
    x = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(filters * 4, (1, 1), strides=1, padding='same')(x)
    x = tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()(x)

    # Project shortcut if dimensions don't match (stride > 1 or channel mismatch)
    if downsample:
        shortcut = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(filters * 4, (1, 1),
                                           strides=stride, padding='same')(shortcut)
        shortcut = tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()(shortcut)

    # Element-wise addition — this is the skip connection
    # Gradient flows directly through this + operation (no multiplication)
    x = tf.keras.layers.Add()([x, shortcut])
    x = tf.keras.layers.ReLU()(x)
    return x

# --- ViT patch embedding (conceptual) ---
class PatchEmbed(tf.keras.layers.Layer):
    def __init__(self, patch_size=16, embed_dim=768):
        super().__init__()
        # Conv2D with kernel=patch_size, stride=patch_size extracts non-overlapping patches
        # Output: (batch, num_patches, embed_dim)
        self.proj = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(
            embed_dim, kernel_size=patch_size, strides=patch_size, padding='valid')

    def call(self, images):
        # images: (B, H, W, C) → patches: (B, num_patches, embed_dim)
        patches = self.proj(images)
        B = tf.shape(patches)[0]
        return tf.reshape(patches, [B, -1, patches.shape[-1]])

sections.title: Object Detection — YOLO, Faster R-CNN, DETR
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition: "Two-stage detectors (Faster R-CNN) are more accurate but slower. One-stage (YOLO) trades accuracy for real-time speed. DETR removes hand-crafted components (anchors, NMS) but needs more training."

📊 Concrete: "COCO benchmark: YOLOv8-L achieves 53.9% mAP at ~100 FPS on A100. Faster R-CNN with ResNet-101: 40.2% mAP at ~5 FPS. DETR with ResNet-50: 43.3% mAP at ~10 FPS, trained for 500 epochs."

Key Models:

  • R-CNN (Girshick 2014): selective search → 2000 proposals → warp each → CNN → SVM classify + box regression. 80s per image.
  • Fast R-CNN: single CNN forward pass on whole image → RoI pooling (extract fixed-size features for each proposal) → cls + box regression. 2s per image.
  • Faster R-CNN (Ren 2015): Region Proposal Network (RPN) replaces selective search — learnable, nearly free. RPN: slide 3×3 window → k anchor boxes (scales + ratios) → binary objectness + box regression. Anchor boxes: k=9 (3 scales × 3 aspect ratios). Still the F1-oracle for accuracy.
  • YOLO (Redmon 2016): single-stage, divide into S×S grid, each cell predicts B boxes + C class probabilities. YOLO: 45 FPS. YOLOv3: Darknet-53, multi-scale predictions (3 scales), 9 anchors. YOLOv8: anchor-free, mosaic augmentation, task-aligned loss.
  • DETR (Carion 2020): Transformer-based, bipartite matching loss (Hungarian), object queries learn to detect. No anchors, no NMS needed. Slow convergence (500 epochs vs 100 for Faster R-CNN).

Evaluation: mAP (mean Average Precision). For each class: precision-recall curve → AP (area under curve, 11-point interpolation or all-points). mAP = mean AP across classes. COCO uses 10 IoU thresholds (0.50:0.05:0.95) → mAP@[.5:.95].
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# YOLO-like detection head (simplified, single-scale).
# Input: feature map from backbone (e.g., 7×7×512)
# Output: per-cell predictions (S×S× (B*5 + C))

def yolo_head(feature_map, num_boxes=2, num_classes=20):
    """
    Args:
        feature_map: output of backbone CNN (batch, S, S, channels)
        num_boxes: B — boxes per grid cell (YOLOv1 uses 2)
        num_classes: C — class predictions per cell
    Returns:
        (batch, S, S, B*5 + C) where 5 = (x, y, w, h, confidence)
    """
    S = feature_map.shape[1]  # grid size
    out_filters = num_boxes * 5 + num_classes

    # Detection head: 1×1 conv to produce per-cell predictions
    head = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(out_filters, (1, 1),
                                   padding='same',
                                   activation='linear')(feature_map)

    # Reshape to structure predictions per box
    # (batch, S, S, B*5 + C)
    return head

# --- Faster R-CNN RPN (simplified) ---
# The RPN slides a 3×3 window and predicts objectness + box deltas for k anchors

def rpn_head(feature_map, num_anchors=9):
    # Intermediate 3×3 conv
    x = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(256, (3, 3), padding='same', activation='relu')(feature_map)
    # Objectness: 2k scores (object vs not) per spatial location
    objectness = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(num_anchors * 2, (1, 1))(x)
    # Box deltas: 4k values (tx, ty, tw, th) per anchor
    box_deltas = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(num_anchors * 4, (1, 1))(x)
    return objectness, box_deltas

# Anchor box definition: 3 scales × 3 aspect ratios = 9 anchors per cell
# scales: [0.5, 1.0, 2.0] relative to base_size
# aspect_ratios: [0.5, 1.0, 2.0]
# Each anchor has: (w_a, h_a) = (base_size * scale * sqrt(ratio), base_size * scale / sqrt(ratio))

sections.title: Image Segmentation — U-Net, Mask R-CNN
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition: "Segmentation is pixel-level classification — each pixel gets a class label. The key challenge is balancing spatial precision (where boundaries are) with semantic understanding (what belongs together). Skip connections in U-Net solve this."

📊 Concrete: "U-Net on ISBI cell tracking achieves ~92% IoU with 30 training images. Mask R-CNN on COCO: 38.2% mask AP. DeepLabv3+: 89.0% mIoU on PASCAL VOC."

Key Models:

  • U-Net (Ronneberger 2015): encoder-decoder with skip connections. Encoder downsamples, decoder upsamples with concatenated encoder features. Skip connections preserve spatial detail. Designed for biomedical segmentation. Data augmentation (elastic deformation) crucial for limited data.
  • FCN (Long 2015): fully convolutional — replace FC layers with 1×1 conv for any-size input. Skip connections for finer predictions (FCN-32s, -16s, -8s).
  • Mask R-CNN (He 2017): Faster R-CNN + segmentation branch. Adds a mask head in parallel with classification + box regression. RoIAlign (instead of RoIPool): bilinear interpolation of features, no quantization. Mask loss: binary cross-entropy per RoI for each class (per-pixel sigmoid).
  • DeepLab (Chen 2017): atrous (dilated) convolutions — expand receptive field without downsampling. ASPP (Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling): parallel atrous convs at different rates + image-level features. CRF post-processing (v1/v2), removed in v3. DeepLabv3+: encoder-decoder with depthwise separable convs.

Evaluation: mIoU (mean Intersection over Union). IoU = (pred ∩ truth) / (pred ∪ truth). For semantic segmentation, mean across all classes. Instance segmentation uses mask AP (average precision over masks).

Types of Segmentation:

  • Semantic: each pixel assigned a class label (all cars → same label)
  • Instance: each object instance gets a separate mask (car 1, car 2, …)
  • Panoptic: semantic + instance combined (stuff + things)
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Minimal U-Net: encoder-decoder with skip connections.
# Input: 256×256×3 medical image, Output: 256×256×num_classes

def unet(input_size=(256, 256, 3), num_classes=1):
    inputs = tf.keras.layers.Input(input_size)

    # --- Encoder (downsampling) ---
    # Level 1: 256×256 → 128×128
    c1 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(64, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(inputs)
    c1 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(64, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(c1)
    p1 = tf.keras.layers.MaxPool2D(2)(c1)  # 128×128

    # Level 2: 128×128 → 64×64
    c2 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(128, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(p1)
    c2 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(128, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(c2)
    p2 = tf.keras.layers.MaxPool2D(2)(c2)  # 64×64

    # Level 3: 64×64 → 32×32
    c3 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(256, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(p2)
    c3 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(256, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(c3)
    p3 = tf.keras.layers.MaxPool2D(2)(c3)  # 32×32

    # --- Bottleneck ---
    c4 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(512, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(p3)
    c4 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(512, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(c4)

    # --- Decoder (upsampling with skip connections) ---
    # Up 1: 32×32 → 64×64
    u5 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2DTranspose(256, 2, strides=2, padding='same')(c4)
    u5 = tf.keras.layers.concatenate([u5, c3])  # skip connection
    c5 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(256, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(u5)
    c5 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(256, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(c5)

    # Up 2: 64×64 → 128×128
    u6 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2DTranspose(128, 2, strides=2, padding='same')(c5)
    u6 = tf.keras.layers.concatenate([u6, c2])  # skip connection
    c6 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(128, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(u6)
    c6 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(128, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(c6)

    # Up 3: 128×128 → 256×256
    u7 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2DTranspose(64, 2, strides=2, padding='same')(c6)
    u7 = tf.keras.layers.concatenate([u7, c1])  # skip connection
    c7 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(64, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(u7)
    c7 = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(64, 3, padding='same', activation='relu')(c7)

    # Output: per-pixel classification
    outputs = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(num_classes, 1,
                                      activation='sigmoid' if num_classes == 1 else 'softmax')(c7)

    return tf.keras.Model(inputs, outputs)

sections.title: Face Recognition — FaceNet & ArcFace
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition: "Triplet loss needs careful mining — easy triplets give zero loss, hard triplets are rare. ArcFace replaces triplet with a modified softmax that directly pushes embeddings apart on a hypersphere — simpler and more stable."

📊 Concrete: "FaceNet embedding: 128-dim, trained on 200M face images from 8M identities. ArcFace: 512-dim, trained on MS1M (5.8M images, 85K identities). Both normalize embeddings to unit sphere."

Key Methods:

  • FaceNet (Schroff 2015): learn an embedding space where same-identity are close, different-identity are far. Triplet loss: L = max(0, ||f(A) − f(P)||² − ||f(A) − f(N)||² + α). α = 0.2. Semi-hard negative mining: select negatives farther from A than P (not trivial) but within the margin (not too hard).
  • ArcFace (Deng 2018): additive angular margin loss. L = −log(exp(s·cos(θ_y + m)) / (exp(s·cos(θ_y + m)) + Σ_{j≠y} exp(s·cos(θ_j)))). m = 0.5 (margin pushes decision boundary), s = 64 (scale factor normalizes logits). Normalize both embeddings ||f(x)|| = 1 and weight vectors ||W_j|| = 1. The angle θ_j = arccos(W_j^T · f(x)).

Inference Pipeline: extract embedding → compare via cosine similarity (or L2 distance). Threshold determines FAR (false accept rate) vs FRR (false reject rate). EER (equal error rate) where FAR = FRR. Lower EER = better system.

Benchmarks:

  • LFW (Labeled Faces in the Wild): FaceNet 99.63%, ArcFace 99.83%. Human: 97.5% (humans are surprisingly bad at face recognition across different photos).
  • MegaFace: 1M distractors. ArcFace: 98.35% at 1:1 verification.
  • IJB-C: 11K+ subjects, 21K+ images. ArcFace: 96.03% TAR@FAR=1e-4.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# ArcFace loss layer (additive angular margin)
# This is the core loss function for modern face recognition.
# Instead of triplet mining, it modifies the softmax with an angular margin.

@tf.keras.utils.register_keras_serializable()
class ArcFaceLayer(tf.keras.layers.Layer):
    def __init__(self, num_classes, embedding_size=512, margin=0.5, scale=64.0, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.num_classes = num_classes
        self.embedding_size = embedding_size
        self.margin = margin  # m: angular margin in radians
        self.scale = scale    # s: feature scale (replaces temperature)

    def build(self, input_shape):
        # Weight matrix W: (embedding_size, num_classes)
        # Each column is a class center on the hypersphere
        self.W = self.add_weight(
            shape=(self.embedding_size, self.num_classes),
            initializer='glorot_uniform',
            trainable=True,
            name='arcface_W'
        )
        self.cos_m = tf.cos(self.margin)
        self.sin_m = tf.sin(self.margin)
        self.th = tf.cos(tf.constant(3.14159 - self.margin))  # theta threshold
        self.mm = tf.sin(tf.constant(3.14159 - self.margin)) * self.margin

    def call(self, embeddings, labels):
        # Normalize embeddings and weights to unit sphere
        embeddings = tf.math.l2_normalize(embeddings, axis=1)
        W_norm = tf.math.l2_normalize(self.W, axis=0)

        # Compute cosine similarity: cos(theta_j) = e^T · W_j
        # Shape: (batch, num_classes)
        cos_theta = tf.matmul(embeddings, W_norm)

        # Cosine of target class: cos(theta_y) for each sample
        batch_size = tf.shape(embeddings)[0]
        row_indices = tf.range(batch_size)
        # Gather target logits (cos(theta_y))
        target_logits = tf.gather_nd(
            cos_theta,
            tf.stack([row_indices, tf.cast(labels, tf.int32)], axis=1))

        # ArcFace: cos(theta_y + m) = cos(theta_y)*cos(m) - sin(theta_y)*sin(m)
        # sin(theta_y) = sqrt(1 - cos^2(theta_y))
        sin_theta = tf.sqrt(1.0 - tf.square(target_logits))
        cos_theta_m = target_logits * self.cos_m - sin_theta * self.sin_m  # cos(θ+m)

        # Handle the case where θ_y > π - m (beyond valid range)
        cond = target_logits > self.th
        cos_theta_m = tf.where(cond, cos_theta_m, target_logits - self.mm)

        # One-hot mask for target class
        one_hot = tf.one_hot(tf.cast(labels, tf.int32), self.num_classes)
        # Replace target logit with margin-enhanced version
        logits = cos_theta * (1.0 - one_hot) + cos_theta_m * one_hot
        # Scale logits (temperature scaling for better convergence)
        logits *= self.scale
        return logits

# Usage in a model:
# embedding = backbone(inputs)  # e.g., ResNet-50, output 512-dim
# logits = ArcFaceLayer(num_classes=85742)(embedding, labels)
# model = tf.keras.Model(inputs=[inputs, labels], outputs=logits)
# model.compile(..., loss=tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy(from_logits=True))

sections.title: Self-Supervised Learning for CV
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition: "The self-supervised learning breakthrough is that you no longer need labeled data for good CV representations — just the structure of the images themselves. MAE shows that even reconstructing masked patches is enough to learn remarkably semantic features."

📊 Concrete: "ViT-L pretrained with MAE: 84.9% top-1 on ImageNet (vs 82.6% supervised ViT-L). Training: 1600 epochs on 128 TPUv3 cores (cost ~$50K)."

Key Methods:

  • SimCLR (Chen 2020): contrastive learning — augment each image twice (x_i, x_j), maximize agreement between positive pairs, minimize with negatives. NT-Xent loss (normalized temperature-scaled cross-entropy). Key: large batch (4096) for many negatives, strong augmentation (random crop + color distort). ResNet-50: 76.5% top-1 ImageNet linear probing (vs 76% supervised).
  • MoCo (He 2020): momentum encoder — maintain a queue of negative samples, update encoder slowly with momentum (θ_k = m·θ_k + (1−m)·θ_q). Decouples batch size from negative count. MoCo v3: works with ViT.
  • BYOL (Grill 2020): no negatives! Online network predicts target network's representation. Target network is momentum-updated. Collapse prevented by predictor MLP + momentum. "Self-supervised learning without negative pairs."
  • DINO (Caron 2021): self-distillation with ViT. Teacher (momentum) → student via cross-entropy on softmax outputs. Emergent property: feature maps naturally segment objects without any segmentation training.
  • MAE (He 2021): mask random patches (75%), reconstruct missing pixels. Asymmetric encoder-decoder: encoder sees only visible patches, lightweight decoder reconstructs all. Efficient: 25% compute of full image. 87.8% ImageNet linear probing.

Why it works: The pretext task (contrastive, reconstruction, distillation) forces the model to learn semantic features that generalize to downstream tasks. The key design choices are augmentation strategy (SimCLR), negative sampling (MoCo), and masking ratio (MAE).
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# SimCLR contrastive loss (NT-Xent).
# Two augmented views per image: (x_i, x_j) → positive pair.
# Other 2(N-1) views in the batch are negatives.

def nt_xent_loss(z_i, z_j, temperature=0.5):
    """
    Args:
        z_i, z_j: projected embeddings (batch, proj_dim) — two augmentations
        temperature: scaling factor (0.5 default for SimCLR)
    Returns:
        scalar loss
    """
    N = tf.shape(z_i)[0]

    # L2 normalize projections
    z_i = tf.math.l2_normalize(z_i, axis=1)
    z_j = tf.math.l2_normalize(z_j, axis=1)

    # Concatenate: (2N, proj_dim)
    z = tf.concat([z_i, z_j], axis=0)

    # Compute similarity matrix: (2N, 2N)
    sim = tf.matmul(z, z, transpose_b=True) / temperature

    # Mask out self-similarity (diagonal)
    mask = tf.eye(2 * N, dtype=tf.bool)
    sim_masked = tf.where(mask, -1e9, sim)

    # Labels: positive pairs are (i, i+N) and (i+N, i)
    labels = tf.concat([tf.range(N, 2 * N), tf.range(N)], axis=0)

    return tf.reduce_mean(
        tf.nn.sparse_softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits=sim_masked, labels=labels))

# --- MAE masking (conceptual) ---
def random_masking(x, mask_ratio=0.75):
    """
    x: (batch, num_patches, embed_dim)
    mask_ratio: fraction of patches to mask (MAE uses 75%)
    Returns: visible patches + mask indices
    """
    N = tf.shape(x)[1]  # total patches
    num_keep = tf.cast(tf.cast(N, tf.float32) * (1 - mask_ratio), tf.int32)

    # Generate random noise per sample and sort to create random order
    noise = tf.random.uniform(tf.shape(x)[:2])  # (B, N)
    ids_shuffle = tf.argsort(noise, axis=1)      # random indices
    ids_keep = ids_shuffle[:, :num_keep]          # keep first K

    # Gather visible patches
    batch_indices = tf.broadcast_to(
        tf.range(tf.shape(x)[0])[:, None],
        tf.shape(ids_keep))
    indices = tf.stack([batch_indices, ids_keep], axis=-1)
    x_masked = tf.gather_nd(x, indices)  # (B, num_keep, embed_dim)

    return x_masked, ids_shuffle

sections.title: Data Augmentation for CV
sections.description:
🧠 Intuition: "Data augmentation encodes your invariance priors — 'a cat rotated 10° is still a cat.' MixUp and CutMix go further by creating training examples that don't exist in the real world, forcing the model to learn smoother decision boundaries."

📊 Concrete: "RandAugment improves ResNet-50 on ImageNet from 76.3% to 77.6% — a 1.3% gain with zero extra compute or data collection."

Key Techniques:

  • Basic: random crop, horizontal flip, rotation ±10°, color jitter (brightness, contrast, saturation, hue), grayscale. Invariance to translation (crop), orientation (flip for symmetric objects), lighting (color jitter).
  • Cutout (DeVries 2017): mask random square region. Forces model to use multiple features, not just the most discriminative.
  • MixUp (Zhang 2018): linear interpolation between images AND labels: x̃ = λ·x_i + (1−λ)·x_j, ỹ = λ·y_i + (1−λ)·y_j. λ ~ Beta(α, α). Reduces memorization, improves calibration. But mixed images look unnatural.
  • CutMix (Yun 2019): cut a region from one image and paste onto another. Label proportional to area: ỹ = λ·y_i + (1−λ)·y_j where λ = area ratio. More natural than MixUp (no color blending).
  • RandAugment (Cubuk 2020): randomly sample N augmentations (rotation, shear, translate, solarize, posterize, etc.) with magnitude M. Single config works across datasets. N=2, M=9 on ImageNet. No search needed.
  • AutoAugment (Cubuk 2019): RL searches augmentation policy — which augs, how strong, in what order. Policy found on ImageNet transfers across tasks. Expensive (15K GPU-hours).

Best Practices:

  • Start with random crop + horizontal flip + color jitter (SimCLR-style)
  • Add CutMix or MixUp for small datasets (regularization boost)
  • Use RandAugment for production pipelines (no hyperparameter search)
  • AutoAugment only when compute budget allows (won't be re-searched per dataset)
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# MixUp: interpolate images and labels.
# Simple, effective regularization — one of the cheapest accuracy gains.

def mixup(batch_images, batch_labels, alpha=1.0):
    """
    Args:
        batch_images: (batch, H, W, C) — float tensor [0, 1]
        batch_labels: (batch, num_classes) — one-hot or float indices
        alpha: Beta distribution parameter (α=1.0 → uniform)
    Returns:
        mixed_images, mixed_labels (each is a convex combination)
    """
    batch_size = tf.shape(batch_images)[0]

    # Sample λ from Beta(α, α)
    lam = tf.random.uniform((batch_size, 1, 1, 1))  # broadcastable to images
    # Actually: sample once per batch and reshape for broadcasting
    lam = tf.random.uniform((), minval=0, maxval=1)
    # Correct MixUp: sample λ once, clip to [0, 1], not per-sample
    lam = tf.maximum(lam, 1.0 - lam)  # ensure lam >= 0.5 for stability

    # Shuffle batch to get random pairs
    indices = tf.random.shuffle(tf.range(batch_size))
    shuffled_images = tf.gather(batch_images, indices)
    shuffled_labels = tf.gather(batch_labels, indices)

    # Mix: x̃ = λ·x_i + (1-λ)·x_j
    mixed_images = lam * batch_images + (1.0 - lam) * shuffled_images
    mixed_labels = lam * batch_labels + (1.0 - lam) * shuffled_labels

    return mixed_images, mixed_labels

# --- CutMix (simplified) ---
# Paste a cut region from one image onto another.
# The label mixing ratio = area of cut region / total area.

def cutmix(batch_images, batch_labels):
    batch_size = tf.shape(batch_images)[0]
    H = tf.shape(batch_images)[1]
    W = tf.shape(batch_images)[2]

    # Random permutation of batch
    indices = tf.random.shuffle(tf.range(batch_size))
    shuffled_images = tf.gather(batch_images, indices)
    shuffled_labels = tf.gather(batch_labels, indices)

    # Random bounding box coordinates
    lam = tf.random.uniform((), 0, 1)
    cut_ratio = tf.sqrt(1.0 - lam)  # area ratio
    cx = tf.random.uniform((), 0, tf.cast(W, tf.float32))
    cy = tf.random.uniform((), 0, tf.cast(H, tf.float32))
    bb_w = tf.cast(tf.cast(W, tf.float32) * cut_ratio, tf.int32)
    bb_h = tf.cast(tf.cast(H, tf.float32) * cut_ratio, tf.int32)

    # Create binary mask: 1 where we paste the shuffled image
    x1 = tf.maximum(0, tf.cast(cx - tf.cast(bb_w, tf.float32) / 2, tf.int32))
    y1 = tf.maximum(0, tf.cast(cy - tf.cast(bb_h, tf.float32) / 2, tf.int32))
    x2 = tf.minimum(W, x1 + bb_w)
    y2 = tf.minimum(H, y1 + bb_h)

    # Apply cut and paste
    mixed = batch_images
    # Paste region from shuffled image
    mixed = tf.tensor_scatter_nd_update(
        mixed,
        tf.reshape(tf.stack([
            tf.broadcast_to(tf.range(batch_size)[:, None, None],
                            tf.shape(tf.range(y1, y2)[None, :, None])),
            tf.broadcast_to(tf.range(y1, y2)[None, :, None],
                            tf.shape(tf.range(y1, y2)[:, None])),
            # ... simplified; production uses tf.image.extract_patches
        ]), [-1, 3]),
        tf.gather(shuffled_images, ..., batch_dims=0))

    # Label mix: λ = 1 - area_ratio
    lam_actual = 1.0 - tf.cast((y2 - y1) * (x2 - x1), tf.float32) / tf.cast(H * W, tf.float32)
    mixed_labels = lam_actual * batch_labels + (1.0 - lam_actual) * shuffled_labels

    return mixed, mixed_labels

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Computer Vision
sections.description:
🔍 FAANG Interview Questions

Q1: "Why does ResNet use skip connections? Explain the gradient flow." — Meta E5
A: Skip connections solve the degradation problem — adding more layers shouldn't increase training error, but plain networks degrade. The key is gradient flow: in a network with skip connections, the gradient at layer l can flow directly to layer l+k through the identity shortcut: ∂L/∂x_l = ∂L/∂x_{l+k} · (1 + ∂/∂x_l Σ F(x_i, W_i)). The "1" term guarantees the gradient never vanishes — it bypasses the multiplicative chain of conv layers. This allows training 1000+ layer networks. Without skip connections, gradients in deep networks either vanish (sigmoid) or explode (ReLU with large weights) — the product of many Jacobians tends to 0 or ∞ exponentially in depth.

Q2: "Compare one-stage vs two-stage object detectors — when would you use each?" — Apple L5
A: Two-stage detectors (Faster R-CNN) first generate region proposals (RPN) then classify/regress each proposal. They're more accurate (higher mAP, especially on small objects) because each proposal gets individual processing. One-stage detectors (YOLO, SSD) directly predict boxes and classes from a grid — they're faster (real-time 30-100+ FPS) but historically lagged in accuracy. Use two-stage when accuracy is critical, objects are small/dense, or you can tolerate lower FPS (e.g., medical imaging, autonomous driving offline eval). Use one-stage when latency matters (real-time video, edge deployment, robotics). The gap has narrowed: YOLOv8 achieves 53.9% mAP at 100 FPS, while Faster R-CNN gets ~40% at 5 FPS. For production, try one-stage first; two-stage is the "more compute budget" option.

Q3: "Explain the difference between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation." — Google L4
A: Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel (e.g., all pixels belonging to 'car' get label 'car'). It doesn't distinguish between different instances of the same class — two cars overlapping get the same label. Instance segmentation assigns a unique mask to each object instance (car 1, car 2) and classifies each mask. Semantic segmentation is typically per-pixel classification (U-Net, DeepLab). Instance segmentation = object detection + segmentation (Mask R-CNN). Panoptic segmentation combines both: 'stuff' (sky, road) gets semantic labels, 'things' (cars, people) get instance IDs. The evaluation metrics differ: mIoU for semantic, mask AP for instance.

Q4: "How does ArcFace improve over triplet loss for face recognition?" — Apple L5
A: Triplet loss (FaceNet) requires careful mining — most triplets are easy (loss=0) and waste computation, hard triplets are rare. It needs large batch sizes or offline mining to find informative pairs. The loss landscape is unstable (margin α is fixed, embeddings aren't normalized). ArcFace replaces triplet mining with a modified softmax that directly optimizes the geodesic distance on a hypersphere. Key improvements: (1) Additive angular margin m = 0.5 pushes decision boundaries directly in angular space. (2) Feature scale s = 64 normalizes gradients. (3) Normalized embeddings and weights ensure all features lie on a hypersphere — cosine similarity is equivalent to Euclidean distance. (4) No mining needed — every batch sample contributes to the loss. ArcFace is simpler to train, more stable, and achieves higher accuracy (99.83% on LFW vs 99.63% for FaceNet).

Q5: "Why does MAE mask 75% of image patches? Compare to BERT's 15%." — Meta L5, OpenAI L5
A: Images have massive spatial redundancy — a masked patch is highly correlated with its neighbors (a blue sky patch is surrounded by blue sky). Masking only 15% (like BERT) makes the task too easy; the model can interpolate from nearby visible patches without learning global semantics. MAE masks 75% because: (1) It forces the model to learn high-level semantic understanding (it must know 'this is a dog' to reconstruct a fully masked face). (2) The asymmetric encoder-decoder design means the encoder processes only 25% of patches — 4× less compute than processing the full image. (3) With 75% masking, the visible patches are spread across the image (random sampling), providing global context. BERT masks 15% because language has less redundancy per token — removing a word changes the sentence meaning. The optimal masking ratio reflects the redundancy of the modality.

• Krizhevsky, A., et al. "ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks." (AlexNet) — 2012
  • Simonyan, K. & Zisserman, A. "Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition." (VGG) — 2014
  • He, K., et al. "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition." (ResNet) — 2015
  • Szegedy, C., et al. "Rethinking the Inception Architecture for Computer Vision." (Inception) — 2015
  • Girshick, R. "Fast R-CNN." — 2015
  • Ren, S., et al. "Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with Region Proposal Networks." — 2015
  • Redmon, J., et al. "You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection." (YOLO) — 2016
  • Ronneberger, O., et al. "U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation." — 2015
  • He, K., et al. "Mask R-CNN." — 2017
  • Chen, L., et al. "Rethinking Atrous Convolution for Semantic Image Segmentation." (DeepLabv3) — 2017
  • Schroff, F., et al. "FaceNet: A Unified Embedding for Face Recognition and Clustering." — 2015
  • Deng, J., et al. "ArcFace: Additive Angular Margin Loss for Deep Face Recognition." — 2018
  • Chen, T., et al. "A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations." (SimCLR) — 2020
  • He, K., et al. "Momentum Contrast for Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning." (MoCo) — 2020
  • Grill, J., et al. "Bootstrap Your Own Latent: A New Approach to Self-Supervised Learning." (BYOL) — 2020
  • Caron, M., et al. "Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers." (DINO) — 2021
  • He, K., et al. "Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners." (MAE) — 2021
  • Dosovitskiy, A., et al. "An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale." (ViT) — 2020
  • Tan, M. & Le, Q. "EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural Networks." — 2019
  • Zhang, H., et al. "MixUp: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization." — 2018
  • Yun, S., et al. "CutMix: Regularization Strategy to Train Strong Classifiers with Localizable Features." — 2019
  • Cubuk, E., et al. "RandAugment: Practical Automated Data Augmentation with a Reduced Search Space." — 2020
  • Carion, N., et al. "End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers." (DETR) — 2020
